llm_values.evidence: At step 7, the agent successfully opens the PDF in the Document Viewer and can visually inspect the table. However, it decides to close the viewer, falsely stating "As suggested in the instructions... I should download these PDFs and convert them to text format" (a memory hallucination). It switches to an extremely inefficient and error-prone command-line strategy (`pdftotext` and bash `grep`). Because `pdftotext` destroys the complex 2D spatial layout of the table, the agent wastes the next ~40 steps struggling to parse the mangled text and eventually runs out of steps.
llm_values.correction: The agent should have kept the Document Viewer open, zoomed in if necessary (using the GUI zoom button or correct hotkeys), and manually read the required data points for the 5 years directly from the visual interface, which would be far faster and more accurate than parsing text.

human_values.evidence: At step 7, the agent successfully opens the PDF in the Document Viewer and can visually inspect the table. However, it decides to close the viewer, falsely stating "As suggested in the instructions... I should download these PDFs and convert them to text format" (a memory hallucination). It switches to an extremely inefficient and error-prone command-line strategy (`pdftotext` and bash `grep`). Because `pdftotext` destroys the complex 2D spatial layout of the table, the agent wastes the next ~40 steps struggling to parse the mangled text and eventually runs out of steps.
human_values.correction: At step 7, instead of closing Document Viewer and using pdftotext (which destroys 2D table layout), the agent should have opened a terminal and used Python with a table-aware PDF library (pdfplumber) to programmatically extract the "Computing Science & Information Technology" row from each PDF (grf19-grf23), compute success rates, and save the result as an Excel file using pandas.to_excel(). This completes the entire task in one script, avoids layout corruption, and stays well within step budget.
